summary: Well-structured PR that correctly centralizes perps connectivity recovery in the background bridge. HIP-3 fallback allowlist and rapid navigation stability verified in browser. Hydration logic is well-guarded against races. Two minor suggestions for robustness under flaky networks.

comments.body: `#hydrateAfterReconnect` guards against concurrent runs via `#isHydrating`, but rapid disconnectâ†’connectâ†’disconnectâ†’connect cycles (common on flaky mobile hotspots) could trigger back-to-back hydrations the moment each prior run completes. Consider adding a minimum cooldown (e.g. 5s since last hydration start) to prevent burst REST calls against the Hyperliquid API under unstable connections.

The comment on line 356-357 correctly documents the single-provider limitation. Good that this is called out â€” when a second provider is added, this cache key logic and the `activeProvider ?? 'hyperliquid'` fallback will need updating.

`fallbackHip3AllowlistMarkets: ['xyz:*']` is the right default for current HIP-3 markets. Note this is a glob that matches ALL `xyz:` prefixed symbols â€” if non-HIP-3 markets ever use the `xyz:` prefix, they'll be auto-included in the fallback universe. The LaunchDarkly override provides the escape hatch, so this is fine as a default.

`REST_HYDRATION_STAGGER_MS = 200` â€” this is a reasonable stagger for separating the market data fetch from the user data fetches. If Hyperliquid rate limits become tighter, this may need to increase. Consider extracting to a shared config if multiple hydration paths emerge.

Good pattern: fire-and-forget with explicit `.catch(() => {})`. The health check correctly avoids blocking the UI on reconnect. The 30s threshold (`MIN_HIDDEN_DURATION_MS`) is a reasonable debounce to avoid unnecessary health checks on brief tab switches.
